content.text: Building a voice assistant requires threading together several specialised subsystems — wake word detection, speech-to-text transcription, natural language understanding, an AI backend, and text-to-speech synthesis — into a pipeline that feels responsive in real time. When the components are co-ordinated correctly the result is an ambient, always-on agent that responds to spoken commands, maintains multi-turn conversations, and integrates with external services such as calendars, smart home devices, and weather APIs.

A voice assistant is a software system that accepts spoken input and returns spoken output, with an AI language model at its core. Unlike a simple command-and-response system, a modern voice assistant maintains conversational state across turns, performs intent classification and slot filling, and can call external tools to answer factual questions or execute actions on connected services.
The complete stack has five conceptually distinct layers:
(synthesising a spoken reply). Each layer has its own latency, accuracy, and resource profile, which means optimising the full pipeline requires decisions at every stage.

Deployments range from cloud-backed consumer devices — where STT and TTS are handled by external APIs — to fully local, privacy-preserving setups running quantised models on a Raspberry Pi or NVIDIA Jetson. The choice of deployment target shapes every architectural decision, from model size to audio I/O library to wake word sensitivity.

Voice is the most natural human communication channel, yet most software still requires hands on a keyboard or screen. A well-built voice assistant removes that friction for scenarios where touch is impossible (driving, cooking, physical disability) or inconvenient (smart home control, quick queries while occupied). For developers, integrating an LLM into the voice pipeline transforms what was previously a narrow command-grammar system into a system that understands free-form natural language.

The LLM backend changes the economics of intent handling. A traditional voice assistant required maintaining an exhaustive catalogue of intents and training examples for each one; the LLM collapses this into a single prompt. The result is far more robust handling of paraphrasing, compound requests, and follow-up questions — without retraining. Tool use extends this further: the LLM decides at runtime which external API to call, eliminating the rigid routing tables of earlier systems.

On the embedded and edge side, the availability of quantised open-weight models (Whisper, Mistral, LLaMA) running at acceptable speeds on ARM hardware means voice assistants can now operate fully offline, removing cloud dependency and eliminating the privacy concern of sending audio streams to third-party servers. This opens medical, enterprise, and industrial use cases that were previously blocked by data-residency requirements.
users can rephrase, ask follow-ups, and use indirect language without the system breaking.
wake word detection uses < 10 MB of memory and < 5 % CPU, so the assistant is available without a button press.
the LLM can call calendar, weather, home automation, and custom APIs, making the assistant genuinely useful rather than just a query–response novelty.
conversation history is maintained across turns, allowing the user to refer to earlier parts of the conversation naturally ("set a reminder for that meeting you mentioned").
the system prompt gives the assistant a consistent voice, level of formality, and personality, which can be adjusted per product or deployment.
faster-whisper and quantised LLMs run on-device, eliminating cloud latency and keeping sensitive audio data off external servers.
speaking response tokens as they arrive can reduce perceived first-word latency from ~2 s to < 700 ms.

each stage adds latency. Wake word detection is < 50 ms, but STT adds 200–600 ms and LLM generation adds 500–2,000 ms. Total round-trip on commodity hardware is typically 1–4 s — noticeable in conversation.
Wake word false positives and negatives:
a sensitivity of 0 produces no false activations but misses many legitimate triggers; sensitivity 1 catches everything but fires on similar-sounding words. There is no universal sweet spot.
STT accuracy drops sharply in noisy environments unless noise suppression (RNNoise, WebRTC VAD) is applied before transcription.
a long conversation history consumes tokens. Naively appending turns eventually exceeds the model's context window, requiring summarisation or rolling truncation.
running faster-whisper base.en on a Pi 4 takes ~1 s per 5 s of audio. A larger model improves accuracy but increases latency beyond acceptable conversational bounds.
cloud STT, LLM, and TTS APIs are priced per character or per second. A heavily-used assistant can incur significant ongoing cost; local processing eliminates this but adds upfront hardware cost.
without careful system prompt engineering the LLM may shift tone across turns or be manipulated by user prompts into behaving out of character.

The voice assistant pipeline executes in five sequential phases on every activated interaction. Understanding the mechanics of each phase is essential for debugging latency problems and tuning accuracy.

A dedicated, always-on thread reads 16-bit PCM audio frames from the microphone at 16 kHz and passes each frame — typically 512 samples (~32 ms) — to the wake word engine. Porcupine processes each frame in under 1 ms on a modern CPU using a pre-trained neural net compressed to run in < 1 MB of memory. When the engine returns a non-negative keyword index, the main loop is signalled. The sensitivity parameter controls the false-accept/false-reject trade-off: a value of 0.5 is a sensible default for indoor use.
Speech capture and voice activity detection

After wake word detection the system begins recording audio frames. A simple energy-based VAD computes the root-mean-square (RMS) of each frame and treats frames above a threshold as speech. Recording continues until SILENCE_FRAMES consecutive silent frames are observed. This produces a WAV segment of the user's utterance with ~0.5 s of trailing silence. For noisier environments, WebRTC VAD (available via the
library) provides a more robust pitch-based detector.

The captured WAV segment is passed to Whisper for transcription. faster-whisper is a CTranslate2-based reimplementation of OpenAI Whisper that runs 2–4× faster than the reference model at the same accuracy. The
model (145 MB) transcribes 5 s of speech in ~200 ms on a modern CPU with INT8 quantisation. The
option removes silent leading and trailing frames, slightly reducing hallucinated content on short clips.

In a classic voice assistant this stage is handled by a separate NLU model (Rasa, Snips, Dialogflow) that classifies the transcript into a named intent and extracts slot values. When using an LLM backend the NLU stage can be collapsed: the transcript is sent directly to the LLM, which infers intent and extracts entities as part of its generation. For higher-throughput systems with constrained latency budgets, a lightweight classifier (distilBERT fine-tuned on a domain corpus) can handle common intents locally and escalate ambiguous queries to the LLM.
The transcript and conversation history are assembled into a messages array and sent to the LLM. Tool definitions are included in the request. If the model returns

, the tool call is executed locally and the result is appended to the history before the model is called again. This agentic loop continues until the model returns a text response. For voice output, the response should be constrained to short sentences: the system prompt

The LLM's text response is passed to the TTS engine. pyttsx3 provides zero-latency offline synthesis using the OS speech engine (eSpeak on Linux, NSSpeechSynthesizer on macOS). For higher audio quality, Coqui TTS or ElevenLabs offer neural voices at the cost of additional latency or API calls. Streaming TTS — speaking each sentence as tokens arrive via the LLM's streaming API — dramatically reduces perceived response latency and is the recommended pattern for production assistants.

attrs.alt: Voice assistant pipeline: Wake Word Detector → Audio Capture → STT Engine → Intent/NLU → LLM Agent (Calendar, Weather, Home Automation tools) → TTS Engine → Audio Output, with Conversation Context store and local vs cloud deployment options

content.text: smart home, automotive in-cabin assistants, industrial HMI, accessibility tooling for users with motor impairments.
always-on agents embedded in devices where a screen or keyboard is absent or inconvenient.
kiosks, hotel concierge devices, and call-centre automation where voice is the expected modality.
building a personal assistant on a Raspberry Pi to learn the full pipeline before committing to a cloud deployment.

Conversely, a voice assistant is the wrong tool when the environment is consistently noisy (open-plan offices, factories), when extremely low latency is required (< 500 ms end-to-end), or when the interaction patterns are so structured that a button or touch interface is faster and more reliable.

The recommended implementation sequence is: set up audio I/O and verify the microphone → integrate wake word → integrate STT and verify transcription → integrate LLM with a simple prompt → add tool definitions → add TTS → tune VAD and sensitivity thresholds → add conversation history management → add error handling and fallback utterances.

# Core audio and ML libraries
pip install pvporcupine pvrecorder faster-whisper anthropic pyttsx3

# Optional: better noise suppression (Linux)
pip install webrtcvad

# Optional: high-quality neural TTS
pip install TTS         # Coqui TTS
Register for a free Picovoice account at console.picovoice.ai to obtain an
. Porcupine ships 22 built-in wake words including
. For a custom wake phrase, train a
model file in the Picovoice console and pass its path as

Run the assistant in a representative environment and print the RMS of each frame to the console. The VAD threshold should be set above the background noise floor but below the lowest expected speech level. Typical indoor values are 500–1,200. The silence frame count controls how long to wait after the user stops speaking: 20–30 frames (~0.4–0.5 s) works well; increase it if users tend to pause mid-sentence.
Conversation history grows unboundedly. A simple strategy is to retain the last
turns (where N = 8–12 is typical) and discard older turns. A more sophisticated approach summarises earlier turns: after every 10 turns, send the history to the LLM with the prompt
and replace the old turns with the summary. This preserves factual context (names, preferences) while keeping token counts stable.

A minimal, five-function script that records five seconds of audio, transcribes it with faster-whisper, sends the transcript to Claude, and speaks the reply using pyttsx3. No wake word, no multi-turn history — useful as a baseline for verifying that each stage works before integrating the full pipeline.

# pip install faster-whisper anthropic sounddevice scipy numpy pyttsx3
import sounddevice as sd
import numpy as np
from scipy.io.wavfile import write as wav_write
import tempfile
import anthropic
from faster_whisper import WhisperModel
import pyttsx3

SAMPLE_RATE = 16_000
DURATION = 5  # seconds to record


def record_audio(duration: int = DURATION) -> str:
    """Record from the default microphone and return a path to a WAV file."""
    print(f"Recording for {duration} s ...")
    audio = sd.rec(
        int(duration * SAMPLE_RATE),
        samplerate=SAMPLE_RATE,
        channels=1,
        dtype="float32",
    )
    sd.wait()
    tmp = tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(suffix=".wav", delete=False)
    wav_write(tmp.name, SAMPLE_RATE, (audio * 32_767).astype(np.int16))
    return tmp.name


def transcribe(wav_path: str) -> str:
    model = WhisperModel("base.en", device="cpu", compute_type="int8")
    segments, _ = model.transcribe(wav_path, vad_filter=True)
    return " ".join(s.text for s in segments).strip()


def ask_claude(text: str) -> str:
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=256,
        system="You are a helpful voice assistant. Keep answers concise (2-3 sentences).",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": text}],
    )
    return response.content[0].text


def speak(text: str) -> None:
    engine = pyttsx3.init()
    engine.setProperty("rate", 175)
    engine.say(text)
    engine.runAndWait()


if __name__ == "__main__":
    wav = record_audio()
    transcript = transcribe(wav)
    print(f"You said: {transcript}")
    answer = ask_claude(transcript)
    print(f"Assistant: {answer}")
    speak(answer)
Example 2 – Full wake word + STT + LLM + TTS pipeline

A complete, production-shaped voice assistant class. Porcupine runs on every audio frame waiting for the wake word. On activation, voice activity detection captures the utterance, faster-whisper transcribes it, Claude generates a reply (calling tools as needed through the agentic loop), and pyttsx3 speaks the result. Conversation history is maintained with a rolling MAX_TURNS window.

A typical home automation voice assistant is deployed on a Raspberry Pi 4 (4 GB RAM) attached to a USB microphone array and a small Bluetooth speaker. At boot, Porcupine, faster-whisper base.en, and pyttsx3 are loaded into memory (total: ~350 MB). The assistant listens for the wake phrase 24/7 while drawing < 300 mW at idle.

When the user says "hey porcupine, turn off the living room lights", Porcupine fires within one audio frame (~32 ms). The utterance is captured (0.8 s of audio) and transcribed in 180 ms by faster-whisper. Claude receives the transcript along with a
tool definition, calls the tool with
, and returns the confirmation text in ~600 ms. pyttsx3 speaks "Living room lights turned off" in ~100 ms. Total latency from wake word to first spoken word is approximately 1.0 s.

Streaming TTS is employed for longer answers. When the user asks "what's the weather going to be like this weekend?", the LLM response is streamed token-by-token and the assistant begins speaking the first sentence while the second sentence is still being generated, reducing perceived latency to under 700 ms.

"""
Production pattern: streaming TTS with token-by-token response
to cut perceived latency from ~2 s to ~0.7 s.
"""
import anthropic
import pyttsx3
import io

def streaming_voice_response(user_text: str, history: list[dict]) -> str:
    """
    Stream Claude's response, speak each sentence as it arrives,
    and return the full response text for history management.
    """
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    tts = pyttsx3.init()
    tts.setProperty("rate", 170)

    full_text = ""
    sentence_buffer = ""
    sentence_ends = {".", "!", "?"}

    history.append({"role": "user", "content": user_text})

    with client.messages.stream(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=512,
        system="You are a concise voice assistant. Speak in natural sentences.",
        messages=history,
    ) as stream:
        for token in stream.text_stream:
            full_text += token
            sentence_buffer += token

            # Speak whenever a complete sentence accumulates
            if token.strip() and token.strip()[-1] in sentence_ends:
                chunk = sentence_buffer.strip()
                if chunk:
                    tts.say(chunk)
                    tts.runAndWait()
                sentence_buffer = ""

    # Speak any remaining fragment
    if sentence_buffer.strip():
        tts.say(sentence_buffer.strip())
        tts.runAndWait()

    history.append({"role": "assistant", "content": full_text})
    return full_text
Blocking the wake word loop with slow STT:

do not run transcription synchronously in the frame-reading loop. Move capture and transcription to a separate thread or asyncio task; the wake word engine must keep processing frames at 16 kHz or it will miss subsequent activations.
Sending the raw audio stream to STT:

STT models expect a bounded, complete utterance, not an open-ended stream. Always use VAD to segment the audio before passing it to the model. Sending the full session audio causes transcription to include everything said since the assistant started.

appending turns without trimming eventually causes token-limit errors or extreme latency as the model processes thousands of tokens of context. Always enforce a rolling window or summarisation strategy.

setting Porcupine sensitivity to 1.0 causes frequent false activations from television audio, similar-sounding words, or background conversation. Default sensitivity to 0.5 and adjust based on observed false-positive rate in the target environment.
Speaking the full LLM response verbatim:

LLM responses often contain markdown formatting (asterisks, bullet markers, backticks) that are audible artefacts in TTS. Strip markdown before sending to the TTS engine, or constrain the response in the system prompt ("Do not use markdown or bullet points.").

if STT returns an empty string (silence, noise, or cough), the assistant must respond with a graceful fallback ("I didn't catch that — could you repeat?") rather than sending an empty string to the LLM, which produces unpredictable output.
faster-whisper base.en outperforms tiny.en on accuracy while remaining fast enough for real-time use. Reserve medium.en for cloud deployments where GPU compute is available.
speak the first complete sentence as soon as the LLM emits it, rather than waiting for the full response. This is the single highest-impact latency optimisation available.
Separate threads for wake word and speech:
run Porcupine in a dedicated thread that signals the main thread via a threading.Event or asyncio.Queue. This keeps the audio pipeline responsive regardless of what the main thread is doing.
Strip markdown from LLM output before TTS:
library converts common formatting to plain text, eliminating spoken asterisks and backticks.
Design the persona in the system prompt:

specify the assistant's name, tone (formal, casual), verbosity ("respond in 2 sentences or fewer"), and what it does and does not know. This produces far more consistent behaviour than relying on default LLM persona.
Add a confidence gate on STT:
, treat the segment as silence and issue a fallback rather than sending low-confidence transcription to the LLM.

store every turn in a structured log (timestamp, transcript, intent, tool calls, response). This data is invaluable for diagnosing mis-activations, improving the system prompt, and curating fine-tuning data.

On a Raspberry Pi 4 with faster-whisper base.en (INT8), typical latency per stage is: wake word < 5 ms per frame, VAD + capture 0 ms (runs in real time), STT ~200 ms for a 3 s utterance, LLM (Claude API) 600–1,500 ms for a 2-sentence response, TTS (pyttsx3) 50–100 ms to begin playback. The dominant latency source is the LLM, which is why streaming TTS is important.

Porcupine uses < 2 % CPU and < 10 MB RAM continuously. faster-whisper base.en occupies ~250 MB RAM and uses 30–60 % of one Pi 4 core during active transcription (typically < 1 s of total CPU time per query). pyttsx3 / eSpeak is negligible. On x86 with AVX2 support, faster-whisper can transcribe in real time at roughly 10× speed, making it viable for continuous dictation in addition to voice commands.
Scaling to multiple users or rooms

For a multi-room deployment, each room requires its own microphone and Porcupine instance (Porcupine is thread-safe and can run multiple instances). The STT and LLM calls can be centralised on a server, with audio VAD running locally per room to minimise network bandwidth. Each session maintains its own conversation history; a Redis-backed session store works well for this pattern.

For cloud-scale deployments (smart speaker back-ends serving thousands of concurrent users), the architecture shifts: wake word detection runs on-device, the audio segment is streamed to a cloud STT service (Deepgram, Google STT), and the LLM is served from a GPU cluster. Horizontal scaling of the LLM inference layer is the primary scaling concern; batching strategies (dynamic batching in vLLM or TGI) provide the highest throughput per GPU.
Free tier, 22 built-in keywords, custom .ppn via Picovoice Console
Deprecated; Porcupine is the maintained replacement
Open-source, offline, requires custom training data
CTranslate2 port of Whisper; INT8 quantisation; best CPU accuracy/speed ratio
Ultra-lightweight (50 MB), ~50 ms latency; lower accuracy than Whisper
~300 ms streaming latency; best accuracy for conversational audio
Wide language support; streaming and batch modes
claude-opus-4-5 for quality; claude-haiku-4-5 for < 300 ms cloud latency
llama.cpp Python bindings; runs quantised Mistral/LLaMA on CPU/GPU
Zero-latency, OS voices; low audio quality
Neural voices offline; ~300 ms on CPU for a sentence
Highest quality neural voices; streaming API; cost per character
Good quality; tts-1 model ~150 ms latency
Picovoice recorder, designed to work with Porcupine frame lengths
PortAudio Python wrapper; suitable for recording and playback
WebRTC voice activity detector; more robust than energy threshold in noise
Domain-specific intent classifier; useful for reducing LLM calls
Real-time neural noise suppression; significant STT accuracy improvement in noise
Constrained domains with known intent sets

Wake word engines such as Porcupine perform all inference locally and never transmit audio. The microphone is always hot, which creates a privacy surface: a user near the device may not realise the microphone is active. Best practice is to provide a hardware mute switch and a visible LED indicator that illuminates when the microphone is powered.

An attacker can craft audio (played from a nearby speaker) containing commands that the STT model accurately transcribes and the LLM then acts upon. Mitigations include: rate-limiting tool calls per session, requiring confirmation before executing high-impact actions ("Are you sure you want to delete the event?"), and filtering transcripts for known adversarial patterns before sending to the LLM.

Audio captured after wake word activation should be held in memory only as long as needed for transcription, then discarded. If conversation logs are persisted for quality analysis, they should be stored in an encrypted store and subject to a configurable retention policy (e.g., 30 days). Users should be able to delete their history on request.

The voice assistant pipeline has five conceptually separate stages — wake word, audio capture, STT, LLM, TTS — each with its own latency and accuracy characteristics; optimise them independently before trying to tune the system end-to-end.

Porcupine is the practical default for always-on wake word detection: it runs entirely on-device in < 5 ms per frame, consumes < 10 MB of RAM, and supports 22 built-in keywords as well as custom wake phrases trained via the Picovoice Console.
faster-whisper with the base.en model and
is the recommended STT backend for local deployments: it transcribes a 3-second utterance in ~200 ms on a modern CPU using INT8 quantisation.

Streaming TTS — speaking each sentence as LLM tokens arrive rather than waiting for the full response — is the single most impactful latency optimisation, cutting perceived first-word latency from ~2 s to < 700 ms.
Conversation context management requires an explicit strategy; a rolling window of the last 8–12 turns is a practical default, with periodic LLM-generated summaries for long-running sessions.
Prompt injection via audio is a genuine attack surface: scope tool permissions tightly, log all tool calls, and require explicit confirmation before executing high-impact or irreversible actions.

Fully local deployments (Porcupine + faster-whisper + llama.cpp + pyttsx3) run on a Raspberry Pi 4 and eliminate cloud cost and privacy concerns, at the cost of higher latency and lower LLM capability compared to the Claude API.
